data.title: Comcast in charts: Domestic wireless lines grow steadily, up ~20% Y/Y in Q1
Dragonfly receives first Japanese patent allowance for solvent-free production
data.description:
Dragonfly Energy (DFLI) up 11.9% pre-market Thursday after saying it received an allowance from the Japan Patent Office for its application for "powderized solid-state electrolyte and electroactive materials," which marks the company's first patent application allowance in Japan.

Dragonfly (DFLI) said the allowed patent application covers innovations in powderized solid-state electrolyte and electroactive materials, a core component of the company's solid-state battery development and dry electrode manufacturing platform.

"This milestone strengthens our global intellectual property portfolio around dry electrode manufacturing and all-solid-state cell development," Dragonfly (DFLI) CEO Denis Phares said.

  • Group BIS Ratio 15.75%, CET1 Ratio 13.63%.

data.title: Honeywell declines after revenue miss, cash flow cut clouds profit beat
data.description:
Honeywell International (HON), the diversified industrial conglomerate spanning aerospace, building controls, factory automation and energy systems, reported results on Thursday that beat Wall Street earnings expectations but missed on revenue, while a reduced operating cash flow outlook appeared to weigh on shares.

Its shares fell 7% in premarket trading. As of Wednesday’s close, the stock was up 11% over the prior 12 months.

First-quarter sales rose 2% to $9.14 billion from $8.93 billion a year earlier, missing analysts’ consensus estimate of $9.28 billion. Adjusted earnings per share rose 11% to $2.45, topping estimates of $2.32 a share.

Net income fell to $821 million, or $1.29 a share, from $1.45 billion, or $2.22 a share, a year earlier.

Some Honeywell’s (HON) weaker metrics included:

  • Revenue missed expectations.
  • GAAP earnings per share dropped 35% year over year to $1.29.
  • Operating income fell 14%.
  • Operating margin contracted 320 basis points to 16.1%.
  • Operating cash flow guidance was cut to $4.4 billion to $4.7 billion from $4.7 billion to $5.0 billion.
  • Current full-year sales guidance of $38.8 billion to $39.8 billion sits below the $39.51 billion analyst consensus at the midpoint.

Portfolio overhaul continues

Honeywell (HON) also said it agreed to sell its Warehouse and Workflow Solutions business to American Industrial Partners in an all-cash deal, continuing a broader portfolio reshaping effort. The unit operates under the Intelligrated and Transnorm brands and generated about $935 million in 2025 revenue. Terms weren’t disclosed.

The company previously announced the sale of its Productivity Solutions and Services business and said the planned Honeywell Aerospace spin-off is now expected on June 29, subject to board approval.

CEO points to orders, backlog

“Honeywell delivered a strong start to the year while navigating a challenging geopolitical environment,” Chairman and Chief Executive Vimal Kapur said in the earnings release.

Orders rose 7%, helping backlog climb to about $38.3 billion. Building automation was a standout, with sales up 11%, while aerospace technologies revenue increased 4%. Industrial automation revenue fell 11%, the weakest segment on a reported basis.

Margins better beneath the surface

Honeywell (HON) said segment margin, an adjusted profitability measure, expanded 90 basis points to 23.3%, helped by pricing, productivity and faster-than-expected stranded cost removal tied to the aerospace separation.

The company makes aircraft engines, avionics, warehouse automation systems, thermostats, fire and safety equipment, industrial controls and specialty materials. Its better-known brands include Garrett legacy turbo technologies, Honeywell Home controls, Intelligrated warehouse systems and Forge industrial software. In defense, Honeywell systems are widely used in military aircraft, tanks, navigation, radar and space applications.

data.title: CSX rallies to a new 52-week high after showing operational improvement in Q1
data.description:
CSX Corporation (CSX) tracked higher on Thursday after reporting higher first-quarter profit and revenue, boosted by strong intermodal volumes and solid pricing trends. Notably, the company's operating margin was 36% for the quarter, up 560 basis points from a year ago. New CEO Steve Angel pushed operational improvements, which led to a 6% reduction in expenses to $2.2B for the quarter.  

Bank of America reiterated its Buy rating on CSX (CSX) and raised its price objective to $49 from $46. Analyst Ken Hoexter said the multiple on the price target is above the rail sector's 5-year range of 18.5X to 21.5X due to CSX's (CSX) accelerating earnings, $150M in costs falling away in 2026, and new management’s operational focus.

Despite the Q1 earnings beat, Morgan Stanley downgraded the rails stock to Equal-weight from Overweight. "Productivity actions are progressing well, and mgmt. is building a pipeline to fill capacity, but at well over 20x PE, this appears more than priced into the stock," warned analyst Ravi Shanker. The firm plans to closely track the pace of productivity gains and pipeline conversion but also sees truck competition intensifying vs. rails and better risk-reward with CSX (CSX) peers.

Shares of CSX (CSX) rose 4.6% in premarket trading to a new 52-week high of $45.17.

data.title: STMicroelectronics projects Q2 2026 revenue of $3.45B as it targets data center revenue well above $1B for 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: STMicroelectronics (STM) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Jean-Marc Chery said Q1 net revenues were $3.1 billion and highlighted that “we had a strong booking momentum during Q1 with book-to-bill well above 1 across all end markets and regions.”

  • CEO Chery framed AI infrastructure as a key growth vector, confirming “our data centers revenue expectation to be nicely above USD 500 million for 2026 and well above $1 billion for 2027,” and adding, “we expanded our strategic engagement with Amazon Web Services through a multiyear multibillion U.S. dollar commercial engagement.”

  • CEO Chery emphasized portfolio and production milestones, including: “we also announced the start of high-volume production for our silicon photonics-based photonics ICs 100 -- PIC100 platform,” and “the first batch of STM32 wafers fully produced in China for ST by our partner, Huahong, has been delivered to customers in China.”

  • Quote (President & CFO Lorenzo Grandi) “Free cash flow was negative at $723 million in the first quarter compared to a positive $30 million in Q1 2025. Q1 '26 free cash flow includes $895 million cash out related to the payment for the acquisition of NXT's MEMS sensor business.”

  • Quote (President Marco Cassis) “I am expecting this one to accelerate, yes.”

Outlook

  • Management guided Q2 2026 revenues to $3.45 billion, plus/minus 350 basis points, and CEO Chery said, “We expect our gross margin to be about 34.8%, plus/minus 200 basis points,” while noting, “This business outlook does not include any impact for potential further change to global trade tariffs compared to the current situation.”

  • CEO Chery described the growth setup for the year without issuing full-year quarterly guidance, saying, “We expect 2026 revenues to show double-digit growth beyond our addressable market dynamics and our already engaged customer programs,” driven by “new AI programs.”

  • Compared with the prior call’s focus on closing the pending NXP MEMS deal, Q1 confirmed completion: CEO Chery said, “In February, we completed the acquisition of NXP's MEMS sensor business.”

Financial results

  • The company reported Q1 net revenues of $3.1 billion and gross margin of 33.8% (34.1% excluding PPA), while CFO Grandi said, “Non-U.S. GAAP diluted earnings per share was $0.13.”

  • CFO Grandi detailed restructuring and acquisition-related impacts, stating, “In the first quarter, we reported $70 million of operating income, which includes $71 million for impairment, restructuring charges and other related phase-out costs,” and “Q1 operating income also included $30 million purchase price allocation effects.”

  • Cash, inventory, and leverage were central themes: CFO Grandi reported inventory of $3.17 billion with “days sales of inventory…140 days,” and said, “ST maintained its financial strength with a net financial position that remained solid at $2 billion.”

Q&A

  • Joshua Buchalter, TD Cowen: Asked whether company-specific drivers could make 3Q/4Q “above seasonal”; Chairman & CEO Chery said he would not guide for 2026, but added, “the billable on '26 from the booking we received in Q1 represent approximately 85% to 90%,” and “we could achieve the usual seasonality H2 versus H1.”

  • Buchalter, TD Cowen: Asked about pricing trends; President & CFO Grandi said, “our expectation is to have a very low single-digit, let's say, price decline,” citing “some selected price increase.”

  • Francois-Xavier Bouvignies, UBS: Asked timing/magnitude of price increases and gross margin implications; President & CFO Grandi said “pricing is quite neutral” for Q1-to-Q2 gross margin and pointed to mix, lower unused capacity charges, and “temporary suboptimal efficiency in the context of our reshaping plan.”

  • Janardan Menon, Jefferies: Asked second-half gross margin “puts and takes” and path to “45%”; President & CFO Grandi said, “the gross margin…will improve sequentially when we look Q1, Q2, Q3 and Q4,” driven by unused capacity, efficiency improvements, and mix, but noted transition benefits “mainly in 2027.”

  • Gianmarco Bonacina, Banca Akros: Asked whether AWS success can extend to other hyperscalers; Chairman & CEO Chery said AWS is “a fantastic driver,” but “our ambition is well above,” spanning “network flow,” “power flow,” and “thermal cooling.”

  • Sébastien Sztabowicz, Kepler Cheuvreux: Asked when reshaping benefits arrive and Sanan SiC JV ramp; Chairman & CEO Chery said Agrate benefits are “more in the end of '27 and entering in '28,” and for Sanan, “we expect to start the production…starting the end of 2026.”

  • Sandeep Deshpande, JPMorgan: Asked NXP MEMS growth history and outlook; President Cassis said the acquired business was “in the range around low single-digit growth,” and said ST expects faster growth through combined capabilities.

  • Lee Simpson, Morgan Stanley: Asked GaN positioning and PIC100 standardization; President Cassis said GaN is “important for the 800 volt,” with revenues “mainly during '27,” while Chairman & CEO Chery said, “we are the unique company capable to provide silicon photonics technology on 12-inch.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated probing on durability of AI-driven upside, pricing, and margin trajectory, including questions on “the potential for upside” and “capacity constraints.”

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, with frequent qualifiers (“I will not guide on '26”) alongside confidence statements such as CEO Chery’s “we saw improving demand with strong booking.”

  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on early-cycle recovery and inventory correction, Q1 carried a more execution-oriented tone around AI program ramps, capacity/qualification constraints, and integrating the completed NXP MEMS acquisition.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 introduced two material updates versus Q4: the NXP MEMS acquisition moved from “expected to close in H1 2026” to completed, and management disclosed an AWS “multiyear multibillion” engagement.

  • Guidance shifted up sequentially from Q4’s Q1 outlook of $3.04 billion revenue and ~33.7% gross margin to Q1’s Q2 outlook of $3.45 billion revenue and ~34.8% gross margin, while both calls kept tariff-change impacts excluded from the outlook.

  • Q&A focus broadened from last quarter’s inventory correction and automotive uncertainty to this quarter’s pricing stabilization, AI capacity constraints, and timing of reshaping benefits (“end of '27 and entering in '28”).

Risks and concerns

  • Management explicitly flagged tariff uncertainty: CEO Chery said Q2 outlook “does not include any impact for potential further change to global trade tariffs.”

  • Execution risk around manufacturing reshaping and transfers remained a recurring constraint; President & CFO Grandi cited “temporary suboptimal efficiency in the context of our reshaping plan,” and CEO Chery pointed to “customer qualification time” as a pacing factor for 300mm and 8-inch transitions.

  • AI demand appears ahead of supply readiness; CEO Chery said “the unconstrained demand we have today for '26 and '27 is well above” the company’s stated expectations, while emphasizing the need to “ramp up…capacity.”

Final takeaway

ST’s management positioned Q1 as an inflection toward AI-led growth, underscored by a newly disclosed “multiyear multibillion” AWS engagement, the start of high-volume PIC100 silicon photonics production, and reaffirmed targets for data center revenue “nicely above USD 500 million for 2026 and well above $1 billion for 2027.” At the same time, executives emphasized that near-term margin progression depends on utilization and mix improvements while manufacturing reshaping and customer qualification cycles constrain the pace of capacity transitions into 2027–2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.description:

  • See BX Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Nasdaq stock up as fintech, index segments drive Q1 earnings beat
data.description:
Nasdaq (NDAQ) was trading higher after an outright first-quarter earnings beat, driven by growth at the fintech and index segments, even as expenses continued to swell.

Shares were 2.41% higher at $88.45 during pre-market trading on Thursday.

The exchange and market data company reported non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $0.96, up 22% year-over-year and beating the average analyst estimate of $0.93.

Net revenue came in at $1.41B, an increase of 14% on a yearly basis. The metric exceeds the consensus by $30M.

Solutions revenue advanced 14% from the prior-year quarter to $1.08B, and market services net revenue rose 13% to $317M.

Annualized recurring revenue was up 13% to $3.2B. Annualized SaaS revenue, representing 38% of the annualized recurring revenue, rose 13% during the quarter.

Financial technology revenue of $517M grew 20% from the first quarter of 2025, while index revenue of $220M increased 14%.

Total operating expenses increased to $750M from $690M in the same period a year ago.

The New York-based company raised its 2026 non-GAAP operating expense guidance to a range of $2.485B to $2.545B from a range of $2.455B to $2.535B.

data.title: Hasbro pops after issuing strong Q1 guidance
data.description:
Hasbro (HAS) rallied in early action on Thursday after providing a guidance update.

The toy seller said its Q1 got off to a strong start, with preliminary revenue of about $970M to $985M, up 9% to 11% from last year, driven by continued strength in the MAGIC: THE GATHERING business. The guidance range is well above the consensus expectation of $909M. The company also reiterated its full-year 2026 outlook, including 3% to 5% constant-currency revenue growth, a 24% to 25% adjusted operating margin, and adjusted EBITDA of $1.40B to $1.45B. 

Hasbro (HAS) also updated investors on the unauthorized network access it previously disclosed, saying the incident appears contained and that Hasbro is making progress restoring systems and operations. Management noted that the issue did not affect Q1 financial results, but it did slow the preparation and filing of quarterly results because some systems were taken offline as a precaution. Hasbro (HAS) said it continues to work with cybersecurity and forensic experts, and it remains able to take orders and ship product, although the Consumer Products segment may see some second-quarter pressure from processing, shipping, and invoicing delays.

Importantly, Hasbro (HAS) noted that MAGIC: THE GATHERING shipments and release cadence have continued as planned in Q2, including the April  release of Secrets of Strixhaven. The company said it expects some delayed Q2 consumer product shipments to be made up in the second half of the year. 

Shares of Hasbro (HAS) soared 8.2% in premarket trading on Thursday to $98.00 vs. the 52-week range of $56.64 to $106.98.
